topic: Someone receives flowers with no card attached
raw_output:
[story 1]

The vibrant crimson roses sat on the kitchen counter, a stark, unapologetic splash of color against the otherwise muted morning. I stared at them, the petals feeling strangely cold beneath my fingertips. There was no note, no whispered sentiment tucked beneath the wrapping—just the blooms, delivered by a delivery service I didn't recognize. My jaw tightened, a muscle jumping near my temple. I picked one up, the stem surprisingly firm, and held it up to the light. It felt like a deliberate, silent statement, an invitation to an explanation that never arrived. I walked to the window, watching the unfamiliar cars pass by, the silence in my apartment amplifying the hollow echo in my chest.

[story 2]

When the package arrived, it was tucked discreetly behind the mail. Inside, nestled in tissue paper, were lilies—so pristine, so aggressively cheerful. I unfolded the paper slowly, my breath catching in my throat. Nothing. No inscription, no signature, just the flowers themselves. A wave of sharp unease washed over me, making my hands tremble slightly as I examined the arrangement. I placed them on the entryway table, not wanting to discard them yet, but the sheer absence of context made my stomach clench. I paced the length of the living room three times, my gaze darting to the door, waiting for someone who clearly hadn't bothered to bridge the distance between us with a single word.

[story 3]

I found them sitting on my desk, a bouquet of pale yellow daisies. They were beautiful, undeniably lovely, yet they felt utterly disconnected from any shared history. I traced the edge of a petal with my thumb, a faint tremor running through my arm. Why this gesture, this grand display of affection devoid of any accompanying thought? A slow, simmering frustration began to build behind my eyes. I picked up my phone, the screen feeling too bright, and scrolled through old messages, searching for any clue, any shadow of reason behind this unexpected offering. The silence in the room seemed to press in, heavy and suffocating.
